does: This paper introduces QuantSightBench, a new benchmark for evaluating large language models on quantitative forecasting tasks using prediction intervals rather than point estimates. The benchmark assesses how well frontier LLMs can provide calibrated uncertainty estimates across continuous quantities in domains like economics, public health, and demographics. The evaluation framework measures both empirical coverage (whether realized outcomes fall within predicted intervals at the correct frequency) and interval sharpness (how narrow the intervals are), providing a more rigorous assessment of uncertainty quantification than traditional binary or multiple-choice formats. Testing 11 frontier models reveals systematic gaps in their ability to produce well-calibrated predictions across confidence levels.
connects: This work builds on a growing line of research examining how LLMs handle reasoning under uncertainty and probabilistic reasoning tasks. It extends beyond existing LLM benchmarks that have focused on judgmental forecasting in simplified formats, drawing inspiration from classical forecasting evaluation in domains like meteorology and economics where prediction intervals have long been standard. The paper responds to limitations in prior work that relied on point estimates or discrete probability assessments, connecting to recent efforts to evaluate LLM calibration and uncertainty awareness. It also engages with the broader literature on how neural models learn to express confidence and uncertainty, building on evaluation frameworks developed for probabilistic forecasting in traditional time series and forecasting contexts.
enables: This benchmark enables future work to rigorously evaluate and improve LLM uncertainty quantification on numerical forecasting tasks, supporting development of models that can reliably estimate confidence bounds for real-world decision-making. The prediction interval framework could drive research into better prompting strategies and fine-tuning methods for calibration. Results suggesting that frontier models struggle with this task point toward opportunities for architectural innovations or training approaches that enhance scale awareness and internal consistency across uncertainty levels. The benchmark may also inspire applications in domains where LLMs could support forecasting workflows by providing credible intervals for continuous quantities, and could facilitate closer integration of LLMs with traditional forecasting methodologies that rely on uncertainty quantification.
